- Do not dimension by customer_id to avoid cardinality blowups; use sampled exemplars for debugging.

### 2) Control plane API latency SLIs
**SLI:** request duration measured at the service boundary (server-side), excluding network time.
- p50/p95/p99 are reported, but SLO targets are p95.
- Separate histograms for endpoint_group={read, write}.

### 3) Config propagation freshness SLIs
We measure freshness in two ways:

A) **Propagation lag SLI (publish-to-apply):**
- **SLI:** distribution of (t_apply - t_publish) per experiment version publish event, per region.
- **SLO targets:** p95 <= 30s, p99 <= 120s.

B) **Staleness SLI (continuous):**
- **SLI:** percentage of router instances where applied_version != active_version for > 5 minutes.
- This is a guard metric; staleness > 5 minutes is treated as SEV-2 if impacting more than one region.

Data sources:
- experiment-service emits config version and publish timestamps.
- routers emit periodic “applied config version” heartbeats with region + instance id.
- policy-engine exposes distribution pipeline health metrics and last-seen version by region.

### 4) Safe default usage SLI

This should remain very low. Elevated values indicate either:

- evaluation failures in policy engine/router
- excessive cardinality filtering causing drop (see metrics tagging restrictions)

| Experiment API availability slow burn (read) | SEV-3 page (business hours) | burn rate > 3x over 6h AND > 1x over 3d | Eng-SRE on-call | Use to catch creeping issues |
| Experiment API availability (write) elevated 5xx | SEV-2 page | error rate > 0.5% over 10m AND request volume > threshold | Eng-SRE on-call + Platform on-call | Mutations failing blocks operations |
| GET latency regression | SEV-3 ticket | p95 > 600ms over 30m | Eng-SRE + owning team | Not an immediate page unless paired with errors |
| Propagation lag p99 breach (per region) | SEV-2 page | p99 lag > 120s for 15m | Eng-SRE on-call + Platform on-call | Region-scoped; pages if any prod region breaches |
| Propagation lag extreme | SEV-1 page | p95 lag > 300s for 10m OR any region has no applies for > 10m | Eng-SRE on-call + Incident Commander | Treat as control-plane safety incident |
| Router staleness > 5m | SEV-2 page | > 5% of router instances in region stale > 5m for 15m | Eng-SRE + Routing on-call | Often indicates distribution channel failure |
| Safe default usage elevated | SEV-2 page | > 0.5% requests using safe defaults for 10m | Eng-SRE + Routing on-call | Pairs with attribution drop investigations |

Runbook links:
- Experiment assignment debugging: see “experiment-assignment-debugging-runbook”
- Propagation lag investigation: see dashboard “experiment-health-dashboards-spec” (Propagation section)

## On-call expectations and escalation
### Primary on-call
- Eng-SRE on-call is first responder for pages tied to these SLOs.

### Owning engineering escalation
- Platform Control Plane / Policy Engine: **Ethan Park** (lead), **Jordan Lee** (assignment), **Benji Okafor** (config services)
- Routing/Data plane: **Vikram Singh** (integration lead), **Amara Diallo** (gateway hooks)
- Telemetry attribution (if safe-default usage is driven by schema/drop): **Logan Wright**, **Caleb Johnson**

### Incident expectations
- SEV-1/2: acknowledge within 5 minutes.
- If propagation lag is impacting multiple regions or blocking stop/rollback actions, immediately escalate to Incident Commander.

### Post-incident
- SEV-1/2 incidents require:
  - incident timeline
  - identified failure mode (e.g., cache invalidation, distribution fanout, schema mismatch)
  - corrective actions with owners
  - alert tuning follow-up if paging was noisy or delayed

## Operational guardrails (service behavior requirements)
The system must degrade safely under control plane failure.

Required behaviors:
1) **Routers must continue serving** when experiment-service is unavailable.
2) If config is missing/unavailable, routers default to **control behavior** (no experiment) and must not crash-loop.
3) **Stop semantics** (start/stop/rollback) must be resilient:
   - If stop cannot be applied due to propagation issues, on-call must have a manual mitigation path (disable experiment flag at policy-engine/router layer).
4) All control-plane mutations must emit **audit events** (see “experiment-config-audit-events”).

## Release gating checklist (SRE)
Before expanding beta or enabling GA defaults in a new region, confirm:
- SLO dashboards exist and have 14 days of stable data.
- Propagation lag p99 <= 120s in the target region for 7 consecutive days.
- Safe default usage <= 0.10% for 7 consecutive days.
- Runbooks are up to date and linked from alerts.
- Kill switch is tested (feature flag disabling route experiments evaluation).

## Change management notes
- Changes that affect metrics dimensions or cardinality (e.g., adding tags for experiment_id/variant_id) require SRE review due to downstream reliability risk (metrics pipeline instability can mask SLO violations).
- Schema changes for experiment config must be backwards compatible for at least one release train; migrations must not break router apply.

## Appendix: Key dashboards
- Route Experiments / Control Plane Overview
  - API availability and latency by region
  - 5xx breakdown and top error types
- Route Experiments / Propagation Freshness
  - publish-to-apply lag p50/p95/p99 by region
  - stale router instances and last-applied version
- Route Experiments / Safe Defaults & Attribution Health
  - safe default usage rate
  - attribution drop rate (for cross-correlation)

(See “experiment-health-dashboards-spec” for canonical panel definitions and alert thresholds.)

source.title: Cache Efficiency Guardrail SLO Proposal (Prefix + KV Cache)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.content:
## Summary
This page proposes **internal cache-efficiency guardrail SLOs** for Redwood’s prefix cache and KV cache telemetry. These are **not customer-facing SLAs**. The intent is to:

- Detect regressions that materially increase cost/latency (e.g., prefix cache disabled, version mismatch causing systematic misses, eviction pressure spikes).
- Provide consistent triage signals for SRE + Runtime + Telemetry.
- Avoid paging on **expected workload shifts** (prompt mix changes, model/route traffic migrations, new customer onboarding).

Guardrail SLOs are evaluated per environment (prod, canary), deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), and region with strict cardinality controls.

## Background / Why now
We are rolling out new cache telemetry (hit/miss counters, miss-reason taxonomy, eviction/pressure indicators, and wasted prefill). This telemetry enables us to:

- Separate “cache got worse” from “workload changed.”
- Attribute drops to actionable reasons (capacity pressure, TTL expired, policy disabled, hash mismatch, version mismatch).
- Set conservative, low-noise alerts tied to burn-rate style evaluation.

## Non-goals
- This does not define any contractual or customer-reported SLA.
- This does not require new eviction algorithms or cache policy rewrites.
- This does not attempt to enforce a single hit-rate target across all workloads; targets are **guardrails + anomaly detection**.

## Terms and definitions
**Prefix cache:** Cache of canonicalized prompt prefixes used to accelerate prefill for repeated/overlapping prompts.

**KV cache reuse:** Reuse of attention KV states across turns/requests where compatible (implementation-dependent; see ADR).

**Wasted prefill:** Prefill tokens computed that do not contribute to subsequent decode speedups due to cache miss/eviction/disabled policy. Wasted prefill is a leading indicator of cost regressions even when latency impact is masked by batching.

**Guardrail SLO:** Internal target with operational response policies (page/ticket/observe), intended to prevent silent unit-cost regressions.

## Proposed SLIs (signals)
The SLIs below are chosen to be:
- Interpretable and joinable with route/model context.
- Low-cardinality (bucketed by request shape; allowlisted route/model identifiers).
- Robust to sampling (ratio metrics + volume gates).

### SLI 1: Prefix Cache Hit Rate (token-weighted)
**Definition:**
- Numerator: prefix cache *reused tokens* (or reused prefix span) attributed to requests.
- Denominator: eligible prefill tokens (tokens that could have been accelerated by prefix cache given policy + canonicalization).

**Why token-weighted:** It aligns with cost impact (prefill dominates GPU time for long prompts).

**Primary breakdowns (guardrailed):**
- model_family, model_version (allowlisted), region, deployment_mode
- route_group (hashed/allowlisted), prompt_length_bucket

### SLI 2: KV Cache Reuse Effectiveness
**Definition:**
- Numerator: KV cache reused tokens/steps (as defined in ADR; excludes retries double-counted)
- Denominator: eligible decode tokens/steps

**Notes:** KV reuse is workload-specific; thresholds should be conservative and primarily used for **regression detection** and **miss-reason diagnosis**, not absolute performance scoring.

### SLI 3: Wasted Prefill Ratio
**Definition:**
- Numerator: wasted_prefill_tokens
- Denominator: total_prefill_tokens

**Interpretation:** Wasted prefill rising indicates we are doing expensive prefill work without reuse benefits (common causes: eviction pressure, policy disabled, canonicalization/hash mismatch, version mismatch).

### SLI 4: Eviction / Pressure Indicators (supporting, not SLO’d)
These are supporting signals used to classify incidents and tune recommendations:
- eviction_events_rate (by eviction_reason)
- prefix_cache_bytes_used / max_prefix_cache_bytes (utilization)
- kv_cache_pressure_gauge (normalized)

We do not set a single global SLO on these gauges; instead they are used as alert qualifiers and to suggest remediation (increase cache size, adjust TTL, routing locality, etc.).

## Proposed guardrail targets (initial)
These initial targets are intentionally conservative and should be revisited after 2–4 weeks of production baselining.

### Global (prod) guardrails
These evaluate at the service level per region + deployment mode, with traffic volume gates.

1) **Prefix Hit Rate (token-weighted)**
- Guardrail: Do not regress by more than **20% relative** from trailing 7-day baseline for the same region + deployment_mode + model_family.
- Absolute floor (only if eligibility is high): if eligible_prefill_tokens_per_5m is high and hit rate < **0.10**, treat as a likely regression.

2) **Wasted Prefill Ratio**
- Guardrail: Do not increase by more than **+8 percentage points** from trailing 7-day baseline (same breakdown as above).
- Absolute ceiling: if wasted prefill ratio > **0.35** for 30m with sustained volume, open an incident ticket (page only if paired with cost/latency impact).

3) **KV Reuse Effectiveness**
- Guardrail: Do not regress by more than **25% relative** from trailing 7-day baseline (KV behavior is more variable; regressions should be validated against prompt mix).

### Canary guardrails (release safety)
For canary, we want sensitivity with low blast radius:
- Trigger if prefix hit rate regresses **>15% relative** vs control (same workload) for 15m with adequate volume.
- Trigger if wasted prefill increases **>5 percentage points** vs control for 15m.

Canary failures should block rollout when coupled with:
- p95 latency regression beyond perf-canary thresholds OR
- cost-per-1k-tokens proxy regression (when available) OR
- miss reasons indicate systemic failure (policy_disabled, version_mismatch, hash_mismatch dominating).

## Error budget policy (internal)
We treat the guardrails as “error budgeted” to avoid alert fatigue.

### Error budget windows
- Primary window: **28 days**
- Secondary windows: 7 days, 1 day (for fast detection)

### What consumes budget
A minute consumes budget when all of the following are true:
- Volume gate is met (see below)
- Telemetry quality is healthy (no pipeline drop indicators)
- Guardrail threshold is violated (relative to baseline or vs control)

### Budget actions
- >80% remaining: no action (observe)
- 50–80% remaining: file investigation ticket to Runtime/Platform (non-urgent)
- 20–50% remaining: escalate to oncall lead for prioritization in sprint
- <20% remaining: declare reliability initiative and schedule remediation (may include config changes, routing policy adjustments, cache sizing)

Note: We do not want the “budget” to imply customer SLO compliance; it is used to prioritize engineering work.

## Alerting proposal (page vs ticket)
### Alert classes
1) **Page (P1/P2) only for likely systemic regressions**
Page criteria (must meet all):
- Sustained violation (multi-window) AND
- High confidence signal (volume gate + telemetry healthy) AND
- Strong indicator of platform regression (miss_reason spike in policy_disabled/version_mismatch, or near-zero hit rate with high eligibility)

2) **Ticket (P3) for slow burns / optimization opportunities**
- Relative regressions without clear systemic cause
- Wasted prefill increase without latency impact
- Elevated eviction pressure correlated with specific model/region

3) **Noisy/anomalous (auto-silence)**
- Low volume
- Known maintenance windows
- Known workload migration events (route moved, model rollout in progress) unless canary/control comparison indicates a true regression

### Initial alert rules (recommended)
These are expressed as intent; implementation may vary between Prometheus/Mimir/etc.

A) Prefix hit-rate “near-zero” (page)
- Condition: hit_rate < 0.02 for 15m
- AND eligible_prefill_tokens > threshold (per region, tuned)
- AND miss_reason not dominated by ttl_expired (to avoid paging on short-TTL workloads)
- Response: page SRE oncall + Runtime oncall; link to dashboard + runbook

B) Wasted prefill spike (ticket)
- Condition: wasted_prefill_ratio increases > +10pp vs 7d baseline for 60m
- AND eligible_prefill_tokens above threshold
- Response: create ticket to Runtime + Platform; attach top miss reasons + top affected routes (allowlisted)

C) Version mismatch misses spike (page in Dedicated/Private; ticket in Hosted)
- Condition: miss_reason=version_mismatch share > 0.20 for 30m
- AND model rollout event detected OR model_version cardinality increases
- Response: Dedicated/Private page (risk of customer-visible cost change); Hosted ticket unless correlated with latency regression

D) Telemetry pipeline health (page to Telemetry)
- Condition: cache metric ingestion drop > X% vs expected for 10m (see pipeline runbook)
- Response: page Telemetry oncall; suppress cache-efficiency pages until data quality restored

## Volume gates and anti-noise mechanisms
To avoid paging on workload shifts, the guardrail system uses:

### 1) Volume gating
Alerts only evaluate when:
- eligible_prefill_tokens_per_window exceeds a per-region threshold
- AND request_count_per_window exceeds minimum for stable ratios

This prevents small tenants/routes from causing noisy ratio swings.

### 2) Baseline comparisons keyed to stable dimensions
We compare against trailing baselines on:
- region
- deployment_mode
- model_family (and optionally model_version when stable)
- prompt_length_bucket

We avoid comparing across different prompt-length mixes by using token-weighted ratios and bucketed baselines.

### 3) Miss-reason qualification
We require miss reasons to classify the event:
- policy_disabled / version_mismatch / hash_mismatch: high confidence regression signals
- ttl_expired: often workload/config-driven; default to ticket
- capacity/eviction_pressure: usually infra/config; ticket unless broad and severe

### 4) Change windows / rollout awareness
During planned rollouts (runtime release, model version change, route migration):
- Prefer canary vs control comparisons rather than baseline comparisons.
- Temporarily downgrade some alerts to tickets unless near-zero hit rate is observed.

### 5) Telemetry data-quality gating
If ingestion-side cardinality guards or sampling changes cause partial drops, we suppress cache-efficiency pages until:
- required tags are present at expected rates
- counters/histograms pass sanity checks (no unexpected resets)

## Dashboards and ownership
Dashboards (spec in eng-platform) should support:
- Hit-rate trends + baseline overlays
- Wasted prefill trend + correlation with eviction/pressure
- Miss reason breakdowns
- Top affected model_family/region/deployment_mode

**Owning teams:**
- SRE (alert rules, operational response): Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant
- Runtime (instrumentation correctness, cache behavior): Omar El-Sayed / Sofia Ivanova
- Telemetry (ingestion, cardinality guards, sampling): Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman

## Operational response (runbook integration)
When alerts fire:
1) Check telemetry pipeline health (avoid chasing missing data).
2) Confirm whether change correlates with rollout/migration (release calendar).
3) Use miss reasons to classify:
   - policy_disabled: validate flags/config; check gateway route settings
   - version_mismatch: validate model rollout + routing compatibility; check join keys
   - hash_mismatch: validate canonicalization version; check prompt hashing deployment
   - capacity/eviction_pressure: validate cache sizing, TTL, and routing locality
4) Mitigate:
   - rollback recent runtime/model changes if canary indicates regression
   - adjust cache size/TTL within safe limits
   - adjust routing locality policy (region affinity) if cross-region mix increased
   - open follow-up to Optimize recommendations pipeline if pattern is customer-actionable

See: eng-platform/runbooks/cache-telemetry-pipeline-incident-runbook

## Rollout plan
1) Week 0 (staging + canary):
- Enable guardrail dashboards
- Enable canary vs control alerts (ticket-only)
- Validate volume gates + data-quality gating

2) Week 1 (prod silent):
- Record guardrail metrics; no paging
- Establish 7-day baseline and identify expected diurnal patterns

3) Week 2 (prod ticketing):
- Turn on ticket alerts for wasted prefill + relative regressions
- Iterate thresholds to minimize false positives

4) Week 3 (prod paging, limited):
- Turn on paging only for near-zero hit rate and clear systemic miss-reason spikes

## Open questions
- What is the best global eligibility definition for prefix cache across streaming chat (ensuring retries are excluded) and for batched prefill?
- Should Dedicated customers have per-customer guardrails (restricted dashboards) or remain aggregated by deployment_mode+region?
- How should we incorporate cost-per-token proxies into paging decisions (requires consistent unit cost estimates)?

## Proposed decision
Approve this as the initial SRE policy for cache-efficiency guardrails, with explicit commitment to:
- Conservative paging (only high-confidence systemic regressions)
- Baseline-based thresholds rather than absolute hit-rate targets
- Two-week production baselining before tightening thresholds

## Approvals
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (required)
- Runtime: Sofia Ivanova or Omar El-Sayed (required)
- Telemetry: Nadia Rahman (required)
- Product (inform): Paula Mendes

This page proposes **internal cache-efficiency guardrail SLOs** for Redwood’s prefix cache and KV cache telemetry. These are **not customer-facing SLAs**. The intent is to:

- Detect regressions that materially increase cost/latency (e.g., prefix cache disabled, version mismatch causing systematic misses, eviction pressure spikes).
- Provide consistent triage signals for SRE + Runtime + Telemetry.
- Avoid paging on **expected workload shifts** (prompt mix changes, model/route traffic migrations, new customer onboarding).

Guardrail SLOs are evaluated per environment (prod, canary), deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), and region with strict cardinality controls.

## Background / Why now
We are rolling out new cache telemetry (hit/miss counters, miss-reason taxonomy, eviction/pressure indicators, and wasted prefill). This telemetry enables us to:

- Separate “cache got worse” from “workload changed.”
- Attribute drops to actionable reasons (capacity pressure, TTL expired, policy disabled, hash mismatch, version mismatch).
- Set conservative, low-noise alerts tied to burn-rate style evaluation.

## Non-goals
- This does not define any contractual or customer-reported SLA.
- This does not require new eviction algorithms or cache policy rewrites.
- This does not attempt to enforce a single hit-rate target across all workloads; targets are **guardrails + anomaly detection**.

## Terms and definitions
**Prefix cache:** Cache of canonicalized prompt prefixes used to accelerate prefill for repeated/overlapping prompts.

**KV cache reuse:** Reuse of attention KV states across turns/requests where compatible (implementation-dependent; see ADR).

**Wasted prefill:** Prefill tokens computed that do not contribute to subsequent decode speedups due to cache miss/eviction/disabled policy. Wasted prefill is a leading indicator of cost regressions even when latency impact is masked by batching.

**Guardrail SLO:** Internal target with operational response policies (page/ticket/observe), intended to prevent silent unit-cost regressions.

## Proposed SLIs (signals)
The SLIs below are chosen to be:
- Interpretable and joinable with route/model context.
- Low-cardinality (bucketed by request shape; allowlisted route/model identifiers).
- Robust to sampling (ratio metrics + volume gates).

### SLI 1: Prefix Cache Hit Rate (token-weighted)
**Definition:**
- Numerator: prefix cache *reused tokens* (or reused prefix span) attributed to requests.
- Denominator: eligible prefill tokens (tokens that could have been accelerated by prefix cache given policy + canonicalization).

**Why token-weighted:** It aligns with cost impact (prefill dominates GPU time for long prompts).

**Primary breakdowns (guardrailed):**
- model_family, model_version (allowlisted), region, deployment_mode
- route_group (hashed/allowlisted), prompt_length_bucket

### SLI 2: KV Cache Reuse Effectiveness
**Definition:**
- Numerator: KV cache reused tokens/steps (as defined in ADR; excludes retries double-counted)
- Denominator: eligible decode tokens/steps

**Notes:** KV reuse is workload-specific; thresholds should be conservative and primarily used for **regression detection** and **miss-reason diagnosis**, not absolute performance scoring.

### SLI 3: Wasted Prefill Ratio
**Definition:**
- Numerator: wasted_prefill_tokens
- Denominator: total_prefill_tokens

**Interpretation:** Wasted prefill rising indicates we are doing expensive prefill work without reuse benefits (common causes: eviction pressure, policy disabled, canonicalization/hash mismatch, version mismatch).

### SLI 4: Eviction / Pressure Indicators (supporting, not SLO’d)
These are supporting signals used to classify incidents and tune recommendations:
- eviction_events_rate (by eviction_reason)
- prefix_cache_bytes_used / max_prefix_cache_bytes (utilization)
- kv_cache_pressure_gauge (normalized)

We do not set a single global SLO on these gauges; instead they are used as alert qualifiers and to suggest remediation (increase cache size, adjust TTL, routing locality, etc.).

## Proposed guardrail targets (initial)
These initial targets are intentionally conservative and should be revisited after 2–4 weeks of production baselining.

### Global (prod) guardrails
These evaluate at the service level per region + deployment mode, with traffic volume gates.

1) **Prefix Hit Rate (token-weighted)**
- Guardrail: Do not regress by more than **20% relative** from trailing 7-day baseline for the same region + deployment_mode + model_family.
- Absolute floor (only if eligibility is high): if eligible_prefill_tokens_per_5m is high and hit rate < **0.10**, treat as a likely regression.

2) **Wasted Prefill Ratio**
- Guardrail: Do not increase by more than **+8 percentage points** from trailing 7-day baseline (same breakdown as above).
- Absolute ceiling: if wasted prefill ratio > **0.35** for 30m with sustained volume, open an incident ticket (page only if paired with cost/latency impact).

3) **KV Reuse Effectiveness**
- Guardrail: Do not regress by more than **25% relative** from trailing 7-day baseline (KV behavior is more variable; regressions should be validated against prompt mix).

### Canary guardrails (release safety)
For canary, we want sensitivity with low blast radius:
- Trigger if prefix hit rate regresses **>15% relative** vs control (same workload) for 15m with adequate volume.
- Trigger if wasted prefill increases **>5 percentage points** vs control for 15m.

Canary failures should block rollout when coupled with:
- p95 latency regression beyond perf-canary thresholds OR
- cost-per-1k-tokens proxy regression (when available) OR
- miss reasons indicate systemic failure (policy_disabled, version_mismatch, hash_mismatch dominating).

## Error budget policy (internal)
We treat the guardrails as “error budgeted” to avoid alert fatigue.

### Error budget windows
- Primary window: **28 days**
- Secondary windows: 7 days, 1 day (for fast detection)

### What consumes budget
A minute consumes budget when all of the following are true:
- Volume gate is met (see below)
- Telemetry quality is healthy (no pipeline drop indicators)
- Guardrail threshold is violated (relative to baseline or vs control)

### Budget actions
- >80% remaining: no action (observe)
- 50–80% remaining: file investigation ticket to Runtime/Platform (non-urgent)
- 20–50% remaining: escalate to oncall lead for prioritization in sprint
- <20% remaining: declare reliability initiative and schedule remediation (may include config changes, routing policy adjustments, cache sizing)

Note: We do not want the “budget” to imply customer SLO compliance; it is used to prioritize engineering work.

## Alerting proposal (page vs ticket)
### Alert classes
1) **Page (P1/P2) only for likely systemic regressions**
Page criteria (must meet all):
- Sustained violation (multi-window) AND
- High confidence signal (volume gate + telemetry healthy) AND
- Strong indicator of platform regression (miss_reason spike in policy_disabled/version_mismatch, or near-zero hit rate with high eligibility)

2) **Ticket (P3) for slow burns / optimization opportunities**
- Relative regressions without clear systemic cause
- Wasted prefill increase without latency impact
- Elevated eviction pressure correlated with specific model/region

3) **Noisy/anomalous (auto-silence)**
- Low volume
- Known maintenance windows
- Known workload migration events (route moved, model rollout in progress) unless canary/control comparison indicates a true regression

### Initial alert rules (recommended)
These are expressed as intent; implementation may vary between Prometheus/Mimir/etc.

A) Prefix hit-rate “near-zero” (page)
- Condition: hit_rate < 0.02 for 15m
- AND eligible_prefill_tokens > threshold (per region, tuned)
- AND miss_reason not dominated by ttl_expired (to avoid paging on short-TTL workloads)
- Response: page SRE oncall + Runtime oncall; link to dashboard + runbook

B) Wasted prefill spike (ticket)
- Condition: wasted_prefill_ratio increases > +10pp vs 7d baseline for 60m
- AND eligible_prefill_tokens above threshold
- Response: create ticket to Runtime + Platform; attach top miss reasons + top affected routes (allowlisted)

C) Version mismatch misses spike (page in Dedicated/Private; ticket in Hosted)
- Condition: miss_reason=version_mismatch share > 0.20 for 30m
- AND model rollout event detected OR model_version cardinality increases
- Response: Dedicated/Private page (risk of customer-visible cost change); Hosted ticket unless correlated with latency regression

D) Telemetry pipeline health (page to Telemetry)
- Condition: cache metric ingestion drop > X% vs expected for 10m (see pipeline runbook)
- Response: page Telemetry oncall; suppress cache-efficiency pages until data quality restored

## Volume gates and anti-noise mechanisms
To avoid paging on workload shifts, the guardrail system uses:

### 1) Volume gating
Alerts only evaluate when:
- eligible_prefill_tokens_per_window exceeds a per-region threshold
- AND request_count_per_window exceeds minimum for stable ratios

This prevents small tenants/routes from causing noisy ratio swings.

### 2) Baseline comparisons keyed to stable dimensions
We compare against trailing baselines on:
- region
- deployment_mode
- model_family (and optionally model_version when stable)
- prompt_length_bucket

We avoid comparing across different prompt-length mixes by using token-weighted ratios and bucketed baselines.

### 3) Miss-reason qualification
We require miss reasons to classify the event:
- policy_disabled / version_mismatch / hash_mismatch: high confidence regression signals
- ttl_expired: often workload/config-driven; default to ticket
- capacity/eviction_pressure: usually infra/config; ticket unless broad and severe

### 4) Change windows / rollout awareness
During planned rollouts (runtime release, model version change, route migration):
- Prefer canary vs control comparisons rather than baseline comparisons.
- Temporarily downgrade some alerts to tickets unless near-zero hit rate is observed.

### 5) Telemetry data-quality gating
If ingestion-side cardinality guards or sampling changes cause partial drops, we suppress cache-efficiency pages until:
- required tags are present at expected rates
- counters/histograms pass sanity checks (no unexpected resets)

## Dashboards and ownership
Dashboards (spec in eng-platform) should support:
- Hit-rate trends + baseline overlays
- Wasted prefill trend + correlation with eviction/pressure
- Miss reason breakdowns
- Top affected model_family/region/deployment_mode

**Owning teams:**
- SRE (alert rules, operational response): Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant
- Runtime (instrumentation correctness, cache behavior): Omar El-Sayed / Sofia Ivanova
- Telemetry (ingestion, cardinality guards, sampling): Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman

## Operational response (runbook integration)
When alerts fire:
1) Check telemetry pipeline health (avoid chasing missing data).
2) Confirm whether change correlates with rollout/migration (release calendar).
3) Use miss reasons to classify:
   - policy_disabled: validate flags/config; check gateway route settings
   - version_mismatch: validate model rollout + routing compatibility; check join keys
   - hash_mismatch: validate canonicalization version; check prompt hashing deployment
   - capacity/eviction_pressure: validate cache sizing, TTL, and routing locality
4) Mitigate:
   - rollback recent runtime/model changes if canary indicates regression
   - adjust cache size/TTL within safe limits
   - adjust routing locality policy (region affinity) if cross-region mix increased
   - open follow-up to Optimize recommendations pipeline if pattern is customer-actionable

See: eng-platform/runbooks/cache-telemetry-pipeline-incident-runbook

## Rollout plan
1) Week 0 (staging + canary):
- Enable guardrail dashboards
- Enable canary vs control alerts (ticket-only)
- Validate volume gates + data-quality gating

2) Week 1 (prod silent):
- Record guardrail metrics; no paging
- Establish 7-day baseline and identify expected diurnal patterns

3) Week 2 (prod ticketing):
- Turn on ticket alerts for wasted prefill + relative regressions
- Iterate thresholds to minimize false positives

4) Week 3 (prod paging, limited):
- Turn on paging only for near-zero hit rate and clear systemic miss-reason spikes

## Open questions
- What is the best global eligibility definition for prefix cache across streaming chat (ensuring retries are excluded) and for batched prefill?
- Should Dedicated customers have per-customer guardrails (restricted dashboards) or remain aggregated by deployment_mode+region?
- How should we incorporate cost-per-token proxies into paging decisions (requires consistent unit cost estimates)?

## Proposed decision
Approve this as the initial SRE policy for cache-efficiency guardrails, with explicit commitment to:
- Conservative paging (only high-confidence systemic regressions)
- Baseline-based thresholds rather than absolute hit-rate targets
- Two-week production baselining before tightening thresholds

## Approvals
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (required)
- Runtime: Sofia Ivanova or Omar El-Sayed (required)
- Telemetry: Nadia Rahman (required)
- Product (inform): Paula Mendes

source.title: Reliability Review: Repeated Tensor-Parallel (TP) Timeouts and Stall Events
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.body:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents presented as different customer-visible symptoms (streaming timeouts, elevated p99 latency, occasional 5xx/504 spikes), investigation strongly suggests a shared class of TP stall modes:
- Rank-level stalls inside or around NCCL collectives (silent hangs rather than explicit NCCL errors)
- Scheduler ordering / fairness issues that can starve collectives under mixed prefill/decode
- Edge cases where cancellation and/or KV cache compaction under memory pressure creates rank divergence

This doc consolidates observed signals, contributing factors, and SRE-driven reliability actions (alerts, mitigations, rollout guardrails). It is not a root-cause postmortem for a single event; see the Sev1 postmortem for the largest impact incident.

## Scope
**In scope:** Multi-GPU TP replicas (2–8 GPUs/replica), mixed workload (short+long prompts, streaming decode), sustained concurrency.

**Out of scope:** Model quality, non-TP single-GPU stalls.

## Customer / business impact (aggregate)
- Primary impact: elevated request timeouts (gateway 504 / upstream timeouts), streaming disconnects, and p99 latency inflation.
- Secondary impact: autoscaling churn and replica flapping due to watchdog-like health checks triggering restarts without clear stall attribution.
- Operational cost: repeated on-call interventions (drain/replace replicas, apply concurrency caps) and higher escalation load for Support/CS.

Representative affected workloads:
- Dedicated enterprise accounts with A100 80GB and H100 pools running TP for long-context chat and high token/s throughput.
- Hosted “high-throughput” tier clusters during regional peak windows.

## Incident pattern (high-level)
We bucketed events into a consistent operational signature:
1) One or more TP replicas stop making forward progress (no token emission / no per-rank progress)
2) Frontend sees increased in-flight request duration; streaming responses stall
3) Timeouts spike (request timeout and/or stream idle timeout)
4) Mitigation (drain replica, reduce concurrency, temporarily disable specific runtime paths) restores service

### Notable events (representative)
| Date (UTC) | Env | Symptom | Mitigation used | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2025-02-17 | Dedicated | Sev1: widespread timeouts; multiple TP replicas stalled during decode | Drain/replace replicas; temporary concurrency cap; isolate workloads to non-TP where possible | Postmortem: 2025-02-17 TP decode hang |
| 2025-02-18 to 2025-02-26 | Dedicated | Intermittent streaming stalls at peak load; p99 spikes correlated to TP pools | Concurrency cap + batch guardrails; targeted canary logging | Support tickets + SRE tracking |
| 2025-03-01 to 2025-03-04 | Hosted + Dedicated | Smaller recurrence: “silent stall” clusters without explicit NCCL error logs | Drain/replace + debug counters; instrumented traces | ENG-4823 / dashboard draft |

## What we observed (signals)
### Common symptoms
- Streaming requests stop producing tokens for long intervals (20–120s) while connection remains open.
- p99 latency increases sharply while median remains mostly stable (classic “tail inflation”).
- Replica-level health appears “up” (process responds), but GPU work is not progressing.

### Early indicators we did not have (gap)
- No per-rank heartbeat to prove progress/failure at the rank level.
- No durable attribution to differentiate compute saturation vs NCCL collective wait vs scheduler starvation.
- No standard on-call procedure to capture NCCL debug traces safely for a short window without excessive overhead.

## Contributing factors (hypotheses supported by data)
This section consolidates the most likely contributing factors based on logs, limited NCCL diagnostics, runtime traces, and code review. Final confirmation is owned by the runtime initiative (ENG-4821), but SRE reliability actions proceed on the assumption these are plausible and recurrent.

### 1) Scheduler fairness + collective starvation under mixed prefill/decode
- Mixed prefill-heavy batches can consume scheduling attention and delay required collectives for decode steps.
- Under sustained load, small ordering differences compound; one rank can end up waiting on a collective the others haven’t reached.
- Tail amplification occurs: one wedged replica accumulates in-flight requests, which further increases pressure and queue depth.

Operational signature: queue depth rises and in-flight requests climb, but GPU utilization may remain non-obviously saturated (or may appear “busy” while blocked on sync).

### 2) NCCL stream/event ordering hardening needed at kernel/comms boundary
- A subset of stalls appear consistent with missing or inconsistent stream/event synchronization around collective calls.
- CUDA graphs constraints increase risk: operations captured or replayed without fully consistent ordering can manifest as silent waits.

Operational signature: little/no error logs; ranks appear “alive” but blocked; stall persists until process is restarted or replica drained.

### 3) Cancellation / client disconnect path creates rank divergence
- Streaming cancellation paths can short-circuit one rank’s work while others still proceed to collective boundaries.
- Without carefully coordinated abort semantics, ranks can block indefinitely.

Operational signature: more frequent correlation with high streaming traffic and client disconnect rates.

### 4) KV cache compaction / memory pressure interactions
- Under memory pressure, compaction/fragmentation can trigger divergent control flow or extended stalls.
- This amplifies under long-context workloads and bursty concurrency.

Operational signature: correlation with high KV usage and long context; sometimes preceded by allocation failures or increased memory management time.

## Why detection lagged
### Monitoring gaps
- We alerted on customer-visible outcomes (timeouts) rather than the underlying stall precursors.
- We lacked a reliable “forward progress” metric per TP replica and per rank.

### Runbook gaps
- On-call actions were mostly “restart/drain until stable” without consistent capture of diagnostic artifacts.
- No standard decision tree to choose mitigations (concurrency cap vs disabling kernel path vs switching routing tier) and to validate success.

## Reliability actions (SRE-owned or SRE-driven)
### A) Observability: new metrics, traces, and structured logging
We are aligning on a minimal set of signals to detect stalls early and attribute likely cause.

**Required metrics (TP replica + rank):**
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{replica_id,rank,model,cluster}`
- `runtime_tp_collectives_in_flight{replica_id,rank,collective_type}`
- `runtime_tp_stall_events_total{replica_id,stall_reason}` (reason taxonomy below)
- `runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{replica_id,queue}` + `runtime_scheduler_starvation_seconds{replica_id,phase}`
- Request-level correlation: `request_timeout_total{route,model,cluster}` and `stream_idle_disconnect_total{route,model}`

**Stall reason taxonomy (initial):**
- `COMMS_COLLECTIVE_WAIT`
- `SCHEDULER_STARVATION`
- `COMPUTE_KERNEL_STALL`
- `MEMORY_PRESSURE_KV`
- `CANCEL_DIVERGENCE`
- `UNKNOWN`

**Tracing additions:**
- Include rank id(s), collective counters, and last completed TP step in request spans to correlate “stuck requests” to rank-level progress.

Owners / tracking:
- Runtime instrumentation: ENG-4823, PRs in redwood + observability-pack
- Alertability review: Aisha Bello + Sergio Costa

### B) Dashboards and alerts (new guardrails)
We are adding a dedicated dashboard and paging strategy focused on “stall precursors” rather than timeouts alone.

**Dashboard panels (minimum):**
1) Heartbeat age p50/p95/p99 by cluster + model + GPU type
2) Count of replicas with heartbeat gap > threshold (see below)
3) Timeout rate (504/upstream) with overlay of heartbeat gaps
4) Stall reason counts over time, broken down by model + runtime version
5) Replica drain/restart events (deduped) overlaid with timeouts

**Proposed alert thresholds (initial; tune during canary):**
- Page (Dedicated):
  - `>= 2` replicas in a customer pool with `heartbeat_age_seconds > 30` for `> 2m`
  - Timeout rate exceeds SLO burn criteria AND at least one replica has heartbeat gap > 30s
- Ticket (Hosted):
  - Any cluster with sustained `heartbeat_age_seconds > 30` on `>= 1` replica for `> 5m`

Notes:
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative to avoid false positives during peak load; they will be adjusted once we have stable baselines.

Owners / tracking:
- Dashboard + alert rules: Sergio Costa (implementation), Aisha Bello (thresholds/paging policy)

### C) Immediate mitigations (operational playbook)
Until runtime fixes fully roll out, we standardize mitigations for on-call and customer-facing teams.

**Recommended mitigations (ordered):**
1) **Drain/replace wedged replica(s)** in the affected TP pool
2) Apply **temporary concurrency cap** or reduce max in-flight streams per replica
3) Reduce batch aggressiveness / enable safer runtime defaults (feature-flagged)
4) If repeated: isolate the workload to a known-stable cluster/pool and coordinate a controlled canary with extra diagnostics

**Verification checklist:**
- Heartbeat gaps return to baseline
- Timeout/504 rate decreases within 10–15 minutes
- p99 latency returns to within normal band for that route/model

Owners / tracking:
- Runbook updates and standardization: Tomáš Novák

### D) Rollout guardrails for runtime fixes (Dedicated-first)
We are adding explicit rollout guardrails to reduce risk of “silent regressions” and to ensure we can rollback before widespread impact.

**Guardrails:**
- Dedicated-first canary for affected accounts, with explicit success metrics:
  - Timeout rate reduction
  - No increase in replica drains/restarts
  - No regression in p99 latency beyond agreed budget
- Feature flags default OFF in general availability until canary success
- Mandatory preflight: dashboard is populated and alert rules are in “observe” mode for 48 hours
- Rollback criteria:
  - Any sustained increase in heartbeat gaps
  - Any increase in stall reason events that correlates with customer-visible timeouts
  - Any increase in Sev2+ paging frequency attributable to TP pools

Owners / tracking:
- Release coordination: Connor O'Brien
- Dedicated comms + canary requirements: Paula Mendes + Lauren Bishop

## Decisions / alignment
- We will treat **TP stalls as reliability incidents** even when they do not surface explicit NCCL errors.
- We will page primarily on **forward-progress metrics (heartbeat gaps)** rather than timeouts alone, to shorten time-to-mitigation.
- We will prefer **fail-fast + drain** semantics over indefinite waiting when a TP step exceeds a bounded threshold (see ADR-0127).

## Open risks
- False positives from heartbeat-based paging if instrumentation overhead or sampling gaps produce spurious “stalls.”
- Performance impact from additional stream/event synchronization or tracing fields; must be monitored in canary.
- Customer confusion if new abort behavior surfaces as explicit errors without clear remediation; requires Support enablement.

## Action items (tracked)
| Action | Owner | Target date | Tracking |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Publish TP stall dashboard + initial alert rules in observe-only mode | Sergio Costa / Aisha Bello | 2025-03-08 | Dashboard doc + observability PR |
| Update on-call runbook with a stall triage decision tree + artifact capture steps | Tomáš Novák | 2025-03-10 | runtime-tp-hang-mitigation runbook |
| Implement and deploy per-rank heartbeat + stall reason tagging | Omar El-Sayed / Julian Moore | 2025-03-12 | ENG-4823 |
| Add rollout guardrail checklist to runtime 1.19 release plan (Dedicated-first sequencing, rollback criteria) | Connor O'Brien / Rafael Mendes | 2025-03-12 | ENG-4829 |
| Create Support-facing “what to collect” checklist + interim workaround guidance | Owen Phillips / Rachel Kim | 2025-03-13 | Support Confluence page |
| Weekly reliability review of stall events: correlate heartbeat gaps → drains → customer impact; adjust thresholds | Rafael Mendes | Ongoing | SRE tracking doc + dashboard |

## Appendix A: Suggested artifact capture (on-call)
When a TP stall is suspected and it is safe to do so (no active customer data export; follow security guidance):
1) Capture replica id(s), runtime version, model, GPU type, TP degree, and concurrency settings
2) Snapshot dashboard panels: heartbeat, timeouts, drains, queue depth
3) Enable scoped NCCL/runtime debug logging for a short window only (coordinate via internal helpdesk if required)
4) Drain the replica after capture (do not leave a potentially wedged GPU in service)

## Appendix B: Success criteria for this reliability effort
We will consider the combined SRE + runtime effort successful when (measured over 2–3 weeks):
- Dedicated TP timeout rate returns to baseline and stays within SLO error budget
- No repeat of the Feb pattern of silent multi-replica stalls
- Mean time to detect (MTTD) improves (heartbeat-based) and mean time to mitigate (MTTM) is consistently < 15 minutes for stall events
- A new TP stall signature results in actionable attribution within one on-call shift (not multi-day debugging)
